WJEC Eduqas Language Component 2, Section B — 10 Most Likely Questions + Skeleton Plans (2026)

For: WJEC Eduqas GCSE English Language, Component 2 Section B (C700U20) — 19th and 21st Century Non-Fiction Reading and Transactional/Persuasive Writing. Section B is Q3 + Q4, and BOTH are compulsory. There is no choice. You write two pieces. Each task is worth 20 marks — AO5 (Communication and Organisation) 12 marks; AO6 (Technical Accuracy) 8 marks. Section B total: 40 marks. Target on the page: ~400 words per task (about half the length of a single 40-mark AQA task).

Two compulsory tasks — rehearse to produce TWO ~400-word pieces in the time. This is the structural difference that catches Eduqas candidates out. You are not writing one long essay; you are writing two short, controlled, complete pieces in roughly twenty-five minutes each. The two tasks almost always differ in form: Q3 is typically an article (for a school or college magazine) or a talk to a class; Q4 is typically a letter to a newspaper or an article giving your views on a contentious statement. Rehearse the IUMVCC shell until you can run it twice, fast, in two different registers. A student who can only write one long piece will run out of time and lose a whole task.

Source. The themes below are Sophicly's verified 2026 Eduqas Component 2 forecast, distilled from the verified Component 2 Section B sittings and the board's recurring statement-of-opinion cluster pattern (see The Complete Writer's Craft — Creative Non-Fiction, Verified Past-Paper Writing Prompts section, for the full table of statements, forms, audiences, and paired reading sources). The forecast applies a recurrence-cycle analysis to Eduqas's own cluster pattern plus current-affairs salience for a 2026 candidate.

Statistics and sources. Every numeric claim and named source in the skeleton plans below has been verified against UK government, regulator, charity, or peer-reviewed sources (Ofcom, NHS, Met Office, WRAP, UNEP, Sport England, Mental Health Foundation, UNWTO and similar). Eduqas examiners credit precise, real reference and penalise invented statistics; everything here is real and citeable in exam conditions. Where a plan needs a figure that could not be verified, the claim is written out and marked [VERIFY-NEEDED] — never state an unverified number as fact.

Section A reminder. Section A pairs a 19th-century non-fiction text with a 21st-century non-fiction text on the same theme. The two-source, two-century comparison is the reading half of the paper; the themes that surface there (childhood, travel, education, the natural world, daily life) overlap with the writing themes below, so the same background knowledge serves both halves of the paper.

Variety of openings — no two model answers start the same way. A top-band answer is not just a correctly structured IUMVCC; it opens in a way that is creative and fits the topic. There is no single "correct" opening. The model essays below deliberately use a different hook archetype each time, so you can see the range available to you. The hooks Sophicly teaches: a question, an "imagine" scenario, a metaphor, an anecdote, a shocking statistic, a bold statement, a contrast, an assumption-then-reversal, and repetition. Pick whichever genuinely suits the statement Eduqas prints — not whichever you rehearsed. Forcing a clever opening onto the wrong topic loses more than it gains.

Opening-hook map — what each model answer demonstrates (so you see the full range):

Q Theme Form / shape Hook archetype used
Q1 Screen time / smartphones Q4-style letter to a newspaper Shocking statistic
Q2 Environment / individual action Q3-style talk to a class Extended metaphor
Q3 Consumer attachment / 'things I can't live without' Q3-style article Personal anecdote
Q4 Celebrity influence on teenagers Q4-style letter to a newspaper Assumption-then-reversal
Q5 School / college life advice Q3-style article Contrast
Q6 Community / civic obligation Q4-style letter to a newspaper Bold statement
Q7 Personal heroes / admiration Q3-style talk to a class Imagine scenario
Q8 Health / fitness / wellbeing Q3-style article Rhetorical question
Q9 Education / exam culture Q4-style letter to a newspaper Extended metaphor
Q10 Travel / favourite places Q3-style talk to a class Repetition / anaphora

These are illustrations, not prescriptions. Study the variety; copy the range, not any single example. Each piece still runs IUMVCC underneath. The architecture does not change; the opening does. That variety is what inventive structure (AO5 top band) rewards.

How to use this document. Each entry below gives:

  1. A predicted theme for Eduqas C2 Section B in 2026, with three plausible stem variants (different form / audience / statement combinations).
  2. A frame — the controlling argument that holds the whole piece together.
  3. Three hook options so you can pick the archetype that suits the stem Eduqas actually prints.
  4. A skeleton IUMVCC plan with sentence-level guidance for every beat, sized for ~400 words.
  5. Three counter-argument variants so the strongest objection the printed prompt invites is the one you concede.
  6. A voice and banned-patterns check to run before you submit.

The plan structure stays identical across all ten questions. You rehearse the shell once; on the day, you swap in the surface prompt's audience, form, and statement of opinion — and you do it twice, because Section B asks for two pieces.

Full IUMVCC walkthrough: Sophicly Persuasive Writing Guide.

Memorise this once — the IUMVCC plan shape (sized for ~400 words)

Sophicly's transactional persuasive structure has SIX beats. For a 20-mark Eduqas task each beat is roughly half the length of the AQA 40-mark version. Memorise the beat name, the Eduqas word target, and the dominant move for each one.

Beat Words (~400 total) Sentences Dominant move
I — Introduction 30–60 2 Hook + topic bridge. No facts list. Build an image.
U — Urgency 60–90 2–3 Metaphor-first. Why this matters NOW.
M — Methodology 150–220 6–9 (three sub-points) Each sub-point: point → metaphor → development → evidence. Organic transitions only.
V — Vision 60–90 3 Three escalating sentences painting the better future.
C — Counter-argument 45–60 2 Concede the strongest objection. Refute with one decisive pivot.
C — Conclusion 45–60 2 Circular echo of the Introduction image, or a climactic final picture.

Total on the page: ~400 words. AO5 reward = inventive structure that does not feel formulaic. AO6 reward = controlled vocabulary, sentence variety, accurate SPaG. Because the piece is short, every sentence must earn its place — there is no room for a slack line in 400 words.

The eight Sophicly hooks (pick ONE per piece)

Eduqas reward variety; pick the archetype that suits the audience the stem specifies.

  1. Counterintuitive fact — a number that overturns assumption.
  2. Personal anecdote — a two-sentence concrete memory.
  3. Sensory snapshot — a single image written like a paragraph from a novel.
  4. Imagine scenario — Picture this: + a future or hypothetical moment.
  5. Rhetorical question — a question only the reader can answer.
  6. Bold statement — a claim, no qualifier.
  7. Contrast / paradox — two clauses that crash against each other.
  8. Extended metaphor — a single comparison the rest of the piece returns to.

MADFATHER'S CROPS — the floor, not the ceiling

You need two or three devices working together at every persuasive moment. Single-device sentences score low on AO5. MADFATHER'S CROPS is an aide-memoire — it lists the techniques you should never forget, not every technique you are allowed to use.

The MADFATHER'S CROPS aide-memoire:

  • M Metaphor / A Alliteration / D Direct address / F Facts + foreshadowing / A Assonance / T Triadic structure / H Hyperbole / E Emotive language / R Rhetorical questions / C Contrast / R Repetition / O Onomatopoeia / P Personification / S Simile + sibilance.

Beyond MADFATHER'S CROPS — the wider technique bank. Top-band AO5 answers reach for techniques the aide-memoire does not list. In a 400-word piece you cannot use all of these — pick the two or three that fit the idea and deploy them with control.

  • Anaphora — repeated word at the start of consecutive clauses ("We have the science. We have the funding. We have the time we are not using."). Repetition that builds pressure.
  • Epistrophe — repeated word at the END of consecutive clauses ("Reduce the bag. Repair the shelf. Refuse the bag.").
  • Polysyndeton — repeated "and" connecting items ("The kit and the strip and the seat and the ticket."). Slows the reader; emphasises accumulation.
  • Asyndeton — list with the conjunctions deliberately removed ("Reduce, repair, refuse."). Speeds the reader up; emphasises compression.
  • Parallelism — same grammatical shape repeated ("Move the bell. Move the calendar. Move the system."). Anaphora + parallelism is a top-band combination.
  • Antithesis — two ideas balanced against each other in matched grammar ("The screen gives us everyone; it leaves us no one.").
  • Chiasmus — A-B-B-A reversal ("We made the phones smart; the phones made us absent.").
  • Climax / cumulative listing — list ordered by escalating intensity ("The bedroom dim. The morning lost. The term gone.").
  • Bathos — sudden drop from the elevated to the trivial. Use sparingly; in 400 words one well-placed instance is plenty.
  • Periodic sentence — the main point withheld until the end ("Despite the platforms, the algorithms and the engineered loneliness, childhood survives.").
  • Litotes — emphasis via understated negative ("This is not a small problem.").

The point. A top-band answer does not just use techniques — it picks the technique that fits the IDEA. Anaphora suits mounting pressure; asyndeton suits decisive action; chiasmus suits paradox. Match the technique to the meaning. In a short piece, restraint beats clutter: three techniques used well outscore ten crammed in.

Effects on the Reader — Sophicly's 4-fold framework

Every beat in IUMVCC has a target effect on the reader:

  • I — Focus. What should the reader notice first?
  • U — Feel. What emotion grips them now?
  • M — Think. What argument is reshaping their judgement?
  • V — Feel + Act. What better future moves them?
  • C (counter) — Think. You have heard the objection; here is why it fails.
  • C (conclusion) — Act. The call that lands.

Name the Effect you are pursuing at every beat. Plans that only chase Think (logic) lose AO5 marks for failing to move the reader.

Banned patterns — they cost AO5 and AO6 marks

  • Shows / showing / shown anywhere in the piece. Replace with illustrates, demonstrates, emphasises, reveals, exposes, presents, conveys, signals, enacts.
  • Abstract concept nouns (the importance of, the situation with, the necessity to). Replace with a concrete image.
  • Too many to be verbs (is / are / was / were) — in a 400-word piece, keep them under six. Use active verbs.
  • More than three or four imperatives across the whole piece.
  • Discourse-marker scaffolding (Firstly … Secondly … Finally). Bridge with verbal connectors instead.
  • Triadic structures without parallel grammar.
  • Vague intensifiers (really, very, just, basically).
  • American register (move as a noun, gotten, trash). British English only.
  • No exclamation marks outside quoted speech. No arrows in prose.

Persuasive craft — principles, not quotas

This is creative writing. There are no numeric rules — no fixed number of rhetorical questions, no set quota of anecdotes, no device confined to particular beats. Aristotle warned against persuasion by "scattered persuasive factors" (technique by count); judge by craft instead. The principles below are how to judge a choice, never boxes to tick.

  • Integrate the three appeals (Aristotle): ethos (why trust the writer?), pathos (what should the reader feel?), logos (what is the actual reason?) — woven together, not one technique repeated.
  • Fit the piece to its audience and occasion: a speech to peers is not a letter to a Minister. Register is judged on AO5.
  • Concrete beats abstract: one specific image or named person moves a reader more than a general claim ("one boy, twelve, walks two hours for water", not "many people suffer").
  • Clarity is honesty (Orwell): prefer the short word, the active verb, the fresh image; cut every word that does no work.
  • Rhythm at the climax: repetition and three-part structures land hardest where the idea peaks — used by instinct, never to a count.
  • Call to action sits in the Conclusion. Make it specific (write to your MP, switch the bedroom for downstairs), not vague (make a change).

Q1 — Screen time and smartphones for under-16s

Q1 — Screen time and smartphones for under-16s

Why this is likely. Smartphone harm to under-16s is the most overdue cluster on the Eduqas statement-of-opinion frame, and the Section A 19th/21st-century pairing thrives on it: Victorian anxieties about the telegraph and the sensationalist press pair cleanly with a 21st-century screen-time text. Current-affairs salience is high — Smartphone Free Childhood (2024), the Online Safety Act 2023, Jonathan Haidt's The Anxious Generation (2024).

Three plausible stems (Eduqas prints ONE of these shapes):

  1. "No child under sixteen should own a smartphone. Schools should ban them; parents should delay them." Write a letter to a newspaper giving your views.
  2. "Smartphones are not the problem. Parents who never set limits are." Write a letter to a newspaper responding to this statement.
  3. "The phone is a window, not a prison." Write an article giving your views on this statement.

Frame. Smartphones did not invent teenage loneliness; they industrialised it. The Sophicly position is not anti-technology — it is anti-design-without-conscience. The reform target is not the child; it is the design.

I — INTRODUCTION (30–60 words · Focus) — Hook options: shocking statistic ("Ofcom found children aged twelve to seventeen now spend more than four hours a day online — almost half their waking life."); sensory snapshot ("Half-past midnight; the bedroom dark, the face lit blue."); imagine scenario ("Picture every fifteen-year-old handing over their phone for one night."). Topic bridge: design that profits from attention costs childhood.

U — URGENCY (60–90 words · Feel) — Metaphor: every notification is a slot-machine handle pulled in a child's pocket. Extension: small wins, escalating spend, the family table emptying of attention. Evidence: Ofcom 2024 Children and Parents: Media Use and Attitudes. Short pivot: "This is not entertainment."

M — METHODOLOGY (150–220 words · Think) — M1, design not user: regulate the engineers, not the children. "We do not blame swimmers for the riptide; we post lifeguards." Name infinite scroll, autoplay, streaks. M2, school as sanctuary: phone-free classrooms let attention breathe; Smartphone Free Childhood (2024). M3, parents as architects: the charging station downstairs is scaffolding, not punishment; the bedroom is for sleep. Verbal bridges only.

V — VISION (60–90 words · Feel + Act) — "The bedside table holds books. Family meals stretch into conversations the household had forgotten possible. Childhood breathes again."

C — COUNTER-ARGUMENT (45–60 words · Think) — three options — Connection: "Phones connect lonely teenagers to communities they could not find offline." Punishment: "A ban punishes the responsible majority for the few." Trust: "Confiscation loses the trust parents need." Refutation pivot: "There is connection on the platforms, and engineered loneliness around it. Both are real. Only one is profitable."

C — CONCLUSION (45–60 words · Act) — Circular echo: the bedroom at midnight; the thumb stops; the phone goes downstairs. Final line: "Childhood is not a market. Stop selling it."

Model answer — Stem 1 (Q4-style: ~400 words) — letter to a newspaper — opens with a shocking-statistic hook

[I — Introduction]

Sir,

Ofcom's 2024 Children and Parents report found that children aged twelve to seventeen now spend more than four hours a day online — almost half their waking life outside school. We have handed a developing generation a device engineered, in a distant boardroom, to take exactly that attention from them. The design that profits from a child's focus is the design that costs that child's childhood.

[U — Urgency]

Every notification is a slot-machine handle pulled in a small pocket, and every win is engineered, in the end, to lose. The same Ofcom data record a family table quietly emptying of attention: parents scrolling beside children scrolling, each present in body and absent in mind. This is not entertainment. It is extraction, and it runs every night while the household believes it is resting.

[M — Methodology]

The instinct, when something harms a child, is to regulate the child. The instinct is wrong here. We do not blame swimmers for the riptide; we post lifeguards. The reform target is not the fourteen-year-old at midnight; it is the boardroom that decided her sleep was a market. Infinite scroll, autoplay and streaks were built, not stumbled upon. There is a second defence: the classroom. Schools that follow the Smartphone Free Childhood pledge, begun by two British mothers in 2024, report lunch halls talking again and lessons holding for forty minutes rather than forty seconds. And there is a third: the home. Parents are not the police; they are the architects of the household's attention. A charging station downstairs is not punishment but scaffolding, and the bedroom is for sleep, not for the algorithm.

[V — Vision]

The bedside table holds books again. Family meals stretch into conversations the household had almost forgotten were possible. Childhood breathes.

[C — Counter-argument]

Some will say, in good faith, that phones connect lonely teenagers to communities they could not find offline. That is true. There is connection on the platforms — and engineered loneliness around it. Both are real; only one is profitable.

[C — Conclusion]

So I ask, through your pages, for one ordinary change: the phone downstairs, the bedroom dark, the childhood no longer for sale. Childhood is not a market. We should stop selling it.

Yours faithfully, [a parent / a teacher / a sixth-form student]

Alternative stems. The same IUMVCC shell answers Stem 2 (letter rejecting the parents-only blame) and Stem 3 (article on the phone as window not prison) — for the article, swap the formal letter open for a measured-definition or repetition hook.

Voice + banned-patterns check. Slot-machine / extraction metaphor anchored I → U → Conclusion. No shows. ~400 words.

Q2 — Environment and individual action

Q2 — Environment and individual action

Why this is likely. The environmental cluster is overdue at the direct-action level, and it pairs perfectly with Section A's natural-world tradition (Victorian travel and nature writing against 21st-century climate journalism). The 2026 candidate sits inside a decade of record UK temperatures.

Three plausible stems:

  1. "We have run out of someone-else-must-act. Every household is now where the climate is decided." Write a talk to give to your class.
  2. "Personal recycling and meat-free Mondays will not save the planet. The problem is corporate, not individual." Write a talk to your class arguing for or against.
  3. "It is governments, not citizens, who failed the climate." Write an article giving your views.

Frame. Individual climate action without policy is performance; policy without citizen pressure is decoration. Sophicly holds both — the household votes with its choices, but the choice that matters most is the vote at the ballot box.

I — INTRODUCTION (30–60 words · Focus) — Hooks: extended metaphor ("The climate is not a crisis; it is a machine running hot."); bold statement ("There is no household innocent of the climate."); imagine scenario ("Picture 2050 — the summer that was once a record is now a Tuesday."). Bridge: the climate is a posture to take, not only a crisis to feel.

U — URGENCY (60–90 words · Feel) — Metaphor: "The flood is not coming. It is in the hallway." Evidence: Met Office 2022 — the UK passed 40°C for the first time on record; Climate Change Committee 2024 progress report — off track for 2030. Short pivot: "This is the room."

M — METHODOLOGY (150–220 words · Think) — M1, the household: "Your fridge is a small parliament" — meat-free week, thermostat at nineteen. M2, the street: "Pavements are policy waiting to happen" — tree-planting petitions, school-streets schemes. M3, the ballot: "A ballot is the household's longest lever" — pressuring MPs on the 2030 pathway (CCC 2024). Verbal bridges.

V — VISION (60–90 words · Feel + Act) — "The street has trees again. Children walk to a school that learnt to switch its lights off. Britain breathes."

C — COUNTER-ARGUMENT (45–60 words · Think) — three options — Corporate-only; wealth (poorest cannot afford solar); despair (Britain is a fraction of global emissions). Pivot: "The corporation answers to the consumer it cannot ignore; the regulator answers to the voter it cannot avoid. Both move together, or neither moves."

C — CONCLUSION (45–60 words · Act) — Echo the record July; the radio now reads a falling number. Final line: "The climate listens to small rooms. Speak from yours."

Model answer — Stem 1 (Q3-style: ~400 words) — talk to your class — opens with an extended-metaphor hook

[I — Introduction]

The climate is not a crisis; it is a machine — a vast apparatus of ocean currents and warm Atlantic drift and slow-moving forces, pressed hotter every year by industry, exhaust and the small daily theft of the household commute. Behind every headline temperature sits that machine, and a machine running hot has only one end-state if we keep feeding it.

[U — Urgency]

The flood is not coming; it is in the hallway. The Met Office confirmed that in 2022 the United Kingdom passed forty degrees Celsius for the first time in recorded history, and the Climate Change Committee told Parliament in 2024 that the country is no longer on track to meet its 2030 emissions targets. Read that twice: we set our own target and are preparing to miss it. This is the room we are standing in.

[M — Methodology]

And yet the kitchen is also a machine — and the household-scale machine is the unit at which the country is built. Three meals a day, multiplied by millions of households, builds the supply chain the nation drives; a meat-free week shifts measurable carbon out of it, and a thermostat at nineteen lets the power station breathe a little less. Pavements, too, are policy waiting to happen. The school-streets schemes and the residents' tree-planting petitions that looked like noise in their first weeks become, in their later months, the infrastructure a council is forced to recognise. But the ballot is the household's longest lever, and the only mechanism that can shift the whole country back onto the 2030 target. The kitchen and the street do their work; the ballot box does the rest.

[V — Vision]

The street has trees again. Children walk to a school that learnt to switch its lights off. Britain breathes.

[C — Counter-argument]

Some will say individual action is a distraction, that only corporate regulation matters. There is truth in it — and a harder truth beside it: the corporation answers to the consumer it cannot ignore, and the regulator answers to the voter it cannot avoid. Both move together, or neither moves.

[C — Conclusion]

So we throttle the machine from the kitchen, from the street, from the ballot box, repeated every Tuesday until the curve bends. The science is patient; the decade is not. The climate listens to small rooms. Let us speak from ours.

Alternative stems. The same shell answers Stem 2 (talk disputing "only corporate regulation works") and Stem 3 (article rejecting the household-blame frame). The cascading metaphor suits the talk; for the article, tighten the long sentences.

Voice + banned-patterns check. Machine / flood metaphor anchored I → U → Conclusion. No shows. Triadic structures parallel. ~400 words.

Q3 — Consumer attachment: 'things I can't live without'

Q3 — Consumer attachment: 'things I can't live without'

Why this is likely. A perennial Eduqas-shaped prompt — personal, reflective, article-friendly — and it pairs with Section A's 19th/21st-century writing on daily life and possessions. It sits inside the live consumer-ethics debate (single-use plastic, fast furniture) without requiring a heavy statistical load, which suits a 400-word article.

Three plausible stems:

  1. "Write about the things you feel you could not live without — and what that attachment reveals." Write an article for your school or college magazine.
  2. "We own more than any generation in history, and value less of it." Write an article giving your views.
  3. "The things we cannot live without are rarely things at all." Write an article for a magazine.

Frame. The objects we cannot live without expose what we actually value — and the gap between the two is the argument. The reflective surface hides a consumer-ethics point: attachment is real, but the marketing that manufactures attachment is not the same thing.

I — INTRODUCTION (30–60 words · Focus) — Hooks: personal anecdote (two-sentence concrete object memory); bold statement ("The things I cannot live without fit in one drawer."); contrast ("We own everything; we treasure almost nothing."). Bridge: attachment reveals value.

U — URGENCY (60–90 words · Feel) — Metaphor: the modern home as a warehouse of things we forgot we bought. Evidence: WRAP 2022 — around 22 million pieces of furniture discarded in the UK each year; UNEP 2023 — only around 9% of all plastic ever produced has been recycled. Short pivot: "This is not abundance."

M — METHODOLOGY (150–220 words · Think) — M1, the object that holds memory: the inherited watch, the worn book — value that cannot be bought again. M2, the manufactured want: "Marketing sells the feeling and delivers the object." Right-to-Repair Directive (EU, 2024) reframes ownership. M3, the test: keep what you would repair; release what you would replace. Repair Cafés UK. Verbal bridges.

V — VISION (60–90 words · Feel + Act) — "The drawer holds five things, each of them earned. The cupboard no longer apologises. What I own, I would mend."

C — COUNTER-ARGUMENT (45–60 words · Think) — three options — Joy (buying brings pleasure); economy (consumption funds jobs); nostalgia (sentiment is not a moral system). Pivot: "There is pleasure in buying, and emptiness in owning what you cannot remember choosing. Both are real; only one lasts."

C — CONCLUSION (45–60 words · Act) — Echo the opening object. Final line: "Keep what you would repair. Let the rest go."

Model answer — Stem 1 (Q3-style: ~400 words) — article for a school magazine — opens with a personal-anecdote hook

[I — Introduction]

My grandfather's watch stopped working in 2019, and I have never had it mended, because mending it would mean admitting I might one day lose it. It sits in a drawer, silent, and it is the one object in my room I would run back into a burning house to save. Everything else in that room I could replace by Tuesday. That gap — between the one thing and the everything else — is worth thinking about.

[U — Urgency]

The modern home is a warehouse of things we have forgotten we bought. WRAP reported in 2022 that the United Kingdom discards roughly twenty-two million pieces of furniture every year, and the United Nations Environment Programme found in 2023 that only around nine percent of all plastic ever produced has been recycled. We own more than any generation in history. This is not abundance. It is accumulation, and accumulation is not the same as having.

[M — Methodology]

Some objects hold memory, and memory cannot be reordered overnight. The watch keeps no time, but it keeps a person; the worn paperback keeps the version of me who first read it. These are the things that earn their place. Most of what we own, though, is a manufactured want: marketing sells the feeling and delivers the object, and the object is in the bin before the feeling has even faded. The European Union's Right to Repair Directive, agreed in 2024, reframes the question entirely — it asks not what we can buy but what we can keep, requiring manufacturers to offer repairs for years after a sale. That is the real test of attachment. Keep what you would repair; release what you would simply replace. Repair Cafés across the United Kingdom now mend, for free, the kettles and lamps we were told to throw away — and in mending them, they tell us which of our things were ever ours at all.

[V — Vision]

My grandfather's watch stopped working in 2019, and I have never had it mended, because mending it would mean admitting I might one day lose it. Q4 — Celebrity influence on teenagers

Q4 — Celebrity influence on teenagers

Why this is likely. Celebrity and role-model influence is a recurring contentious-statement shape, ideal for the Q4 letter, and it pairs with Section A writing on fame, reputation and public figures across two centuries. Current salience: body-image research and edited-image disclosure law.

Three plausible stems:

  1. "Celebrities are the worst possible influence on teenagers. Schools should stop pretending otherwise." Write a letter to a newspaper giving your views.
  2. "Stop blaming celebrities. The problem is the platforms that monetise their reach." Write a letter to a newspaper responding to this statement.
  3. "Today's teenagers admire the wrong people for the wrong reasons." Write an article giving your views.

Frame. The celebrity is not the problem; the comparison machine built around the celebrity is. A teenager admiring a footballer's discipline learns something real; a teenager comparing her face to an edited one learns to lose. The reform target is the edited image and the unlabelled advert, not the admiration.

I — INTRODUCTION (30–60 words · Focus) — Hooks: assumption-then-reversal ("You have heard celebrities are role models — in fact, most are products."); counterintuitive fact ("The face many teenagers compare themselves to has never existed in the world."); bold statement ("Admiration is healthy; comparison is the harm."). Bridge: it is the comparison that is industrial.

U — URGENCY (60–90 words · Feel) — Metaphor: the celebrity feed as a hall of mirrors built by people the teenager will never meet. Evidence: Mental Health Foundation 2019 body-image report — 34% of UK adults anxious about body image, rising above half among teenagers. Short pivot: "This is not admiration."

M — METHODOLOGY (150–220 words · Think) — M1, the edited image: label it as we label the doctored one; Norway Marketing Control Act (2021). M2, the unlabelled advert: down-rank cosmetic and gambling promotion to under-eighteens. M3, the better admiration: teach teenagers to admire the work, not the face — discipline, craft, kindness. Anchor the argument in the Mental Health Foundation's body-image findings and the Dove Self-Esteem Project rather than a celebrity-influence survey figure. Verbal bridges.

V — VISION (60–90 words · Feel + Act) — "The feed labels what it edits. The teenager admires a craft, not a filter. The comparison loses its grip."

C — COUNTER-ARGUMENT (45–60 words · Think) — three options — Always-was (teenagers always had idols); free-speech (regulation is censorship); agency (teenagers can decide for themselves). Pivot: "There were always idols; there was not always a billion-pound industry editing them. The admiration is old. The seller is new."

C — CONCLUSION (45–60 words · Act) — Echo the hall-of-mirrors image. Final line: "Label the image. Teach the admiration. Give the teenager back the mirror."

Model answer — Stem 1 (Q4-style: ~400 words) — letter to a newspaper — opens with an assumption-then-reversal hook

[I — Introduction]

Sir,

You have heard that celebrities are role models — that the famous faces filling a teenager's feed are people to look up to, examples to follow, ambitions to hold. You have heard that admiration is the natural relationship between the young and the famous. In fact, most of what reaches a teenager through that feed is not a person at all. It is a product, edited and sold, and the comparison it provokes is industrial.

[U — Urgency]

The celebrity feed is a hall of mirrors built by people the teenager will never meet. The Mental Health Foundation's 2019 body-image report found that thirty-four percent of British adults had felt anxious because of their body image, with the figure rising above half among teenagers — and that research predates the acceleration of edited content since. This is not admiration. It is a comparison engineered to fail, repeated dozens of times a day on a device the child carries everywhere.

[M — Methodology]

The instinct is to lecture the teenager. The instinct is wrong. The first reform is the edited image: label it the way we already label a doctored advertisement. Norway amended its Marketing Control Act in 2021 to require influencers to disclose when an image of a body has been digitally altered, and the British teenager scrolling tonight has no such protection. The second reform is the unlabelled advert: cosmetic procedures and gambling reach under-eighteens through celebrity feeds with almost no friction, and that promotion can and should be down-ranked. The third is the admiration itself, redirected. A teenager who admires a footballer's discipline, a musician's craft, an athlete's recovery from injury has learned something true; a teenager comparing her face to an edited one has only learned to lose. We do not need to abolish the famous. We need to label what they sell and teach what is worth admiring in them.

[V — Vision]

The feed labels what it edits. The teenager admires a craft, not a filter. The comparison loses its grip.

[C — Counter-argument]

Some will say teenagers have always had idols, and this is nothing new. There were always idols; there was not always a billion-pound industry editing them. The admiration is old. The seller is new.

[C — Conclusion]

So I ask, through your pages, for the simplest correction: label the image, regulate the advert, and teach the admiration worth keeping. Give the teenager back the mirror.

Yours faithfully, [a parent / a teacher / a sixth-form student]

Alternative stems. The same shell answers Stem 2 (letter on platforms that monetise reach — anchor M2 on the platforms) and Stem 3 (article on admiring the wrong people). The assumption-then-reversal hook works in all three.

Q5 — School / college life advice

Q5 — School / college life advice

Why this is likely. Advice-to-students is a classic Eduqas article shape — direct, warm, audience-aware — and it pairs with Section A's two-century writing on education and growing up. It needs little statistical load, which suits a 400-word article that lives on voice.

Three plausible stems:

  1. "Write an article for new Year 7 (or college) students offering your advice on how to make the most of school or college life." Article for the school or college magazine.
  2. "The best lessons of school are never on the timetable." Write an article giving your views.
  3. "School is not preparation for life; it is life." Write an article for a magazine.

Frame. The advice that matters is not about grades; it is about the two or three habits that decide whether school happens to you or with you. The contrast between the surviving student and the inhabiting student is the whole argument.

I — INTRODUCTION (30–60 words · Focus) — Hooks: contrast ("There are two ways to spend five years here: surviving them, or living them."); personal anecdote (one small early mistake); imagine scenario ("Picture yourself on your last day — what will you wish you had done on your first?"). Bridge: the habit decides the experience.

U — URGENCY (60–90 words · Feel) — Metaphor: school as a building you either pass through or move into. Evidence: NHS / Sleep Foundation 8–10 hours for teenagers (the sleep habit that protects everything else). Short pivot: "This is not about marks."

M — METHODOLOGY (150–220 words · Think) — M1, show up rested: the sleep habit (NHS 8–10 hours) that makes every other habit possible. M2, join one thing: the club, the team, the choir — the room where school becomes friendship. M3, ask the question: the hand raised is the lesson learned. Verbal bridges. (No heavy statistics needed; voice carries this one.)

V — VISION (60–90 words · Feel + Act) — "You leave knowing the building, not just passing through it. You leave with friends, not just qualifications. You leave having lived the five years, not survived them."

C — COUNTER-ARGUMENT (45–60 words · Think) — three options — Grades-only (only results matter); introvert (joining things is not for everyone); cool (trying hard is uncool). Pivot: "Grades matter — and so does whether you have anyone to celebrate them with. Both count; only one is on the certificate."

C — CONCLUSION (45–60 words · Act) — Echo the first-day image. Final line: "Sleep. Join. Ask. The rest will follow."

Model answer — Stem 1 (Q3-style: ~400 words) — article for the school magazine — opens with a contrast hook

[I — Introduction]

There are two ways to spend the next five years in this building. You can survive them — head down, corridor to corridor, counting the terms until they are over. Or you can live them. The difference is not luck, and it is not even cleverness. It comes down to two or three small habits, started early, that decide whether school happens to you or with you.

[U — Urgency]

School is a building you can either pass through or move into. The students who only pass through arrive tired, leave fast, and remember almost nothing five years later; the students who move in are still talking about it at thirty. And it begins, unglamorously, with sleep: the NHS and the Sleep Foundation both recommend eight to ten hours a night for teenagers, and the student who protects that sleep protects every other habit on this page. This is not about marks. It is about which version of these years you get to keep.

[M — Methodology]

So here is the advice nobody printed on your timetable. First, show up rested — a brain that has slept can learn in forty minutes what a tired one cannot learn in a morning, and the phone left downstairs at night is the cheapest grade you will ever earn. Second, join one thing. One club, one team, one choir, one anything — because the room where you do something alongside other people is the room where school quietly turns into friendship, and friendship is what you will actually remember. Third, ask the question. The hand you are too embarrassed to raise is the lesson you are choosing not to learn, and the teacher you are too shy to talk to is the reference you will one day wish you had. None of this is on the certificate. All of it decides the five years.

[V — Vision]

You leave knowing this building, not just passing through it. You leave with friends, not only qualifications. You leave having lived these five years, not survived them.

[C — Counter-argument]

Some will tell you only the grades matter. The grades matter — and so does whether you have anyone to celebrate them with. Both count; only one of them is printed on the paper.

[C — Conclusion]

So, new students: sleep, join, ask. Do those three things from the first week, not the last. The rest will follow.

Alternative stems. The same shell answers Stem 2 (article on lessons not on the timetable) and Stem 3 (article on school as life, not preparation for it). The contrast hook fits all three.

Q6 — Community and civic obligation

Q6 — Community and civic obligation

Why this is likely. Civic duty and community responsibility is a recurring statement-of-opinion shape suited to the Q4 letter, and it pairs with Section A's two-century writing on charity, neighbourliness and public life (Victorian philanthropy against 21st-century volunteering journalism).

Three plausible stems:

  1. "Everyone owes their community a few hours a month. Those who give nothing should expect nothing." Write a letter to a newspaper giving your views.
  2. "Volunteering is not a duty; it is a privilege the comfortable can afford." Write a letter to a newspaper responding to this statement.
  3. "The strong community is the one that does not wait to be asked." Write an article giving your views.

Frame. A community is not a place you live in; it is a thing you maintain. The bystander who waits for the council to act and the resident who acts are not abstractions — they are the difference between a street and a neighbourhood.

I — INTRODUCTION (30–60 words · Focus) — Hooks: bold statement ("A community is not a place you live in; it is a thing you keep alive."); sensory snapshot (the closed library, the overgrown green); rhetorical question ("Who exactly do we think will mend the street, if not the people who live on it?"). Bridge: community is maintained, not inherited.

U — URGENCY (60–90 words · Feel) — Metaphor: a community as a garden — untended, it does not stay still; it returns to weeds. Evidence: NCVO's UK Civil Society Almanac (2024) — formal monthly volunteering in England has fallen to 16%, from 23% before the pandemic. Short pivot: "This is not someone else's job."

M — METHODOLOGY (150–220 words · Think) — M1, the few hours: the litter-pick, the reading-helper, the lift to the surgery — small, repeatable, real. M2, the young hands: youth volunteering builds the habit early; school-linked schemes. M3, the institution that needs us: the library, the food bank, the grassroots club that survives on volunteers. (NCVO 2024: monthly volunteering down to 16% from 23% pre-pandemic.) Verbal bridges.

V — VISION (60–90 words · Feel + Act) — "The library opens on Saturdays again. The green is mown by the people who walk on it. The street remembers it is a neighbourhood."

C — COUNTER-ARGUMENT (45–60 words · Think) — three options — Privilege (only the comfortable can spare time); state (this is the council's job, not ours); coercion (obligation kills generosity). Pivot: "It is true that not everyone can give hours. It is also true that a community waiting only for the council is a community already half-empty. Both are real; only one is a plan."

C — CONCLUSION (45–60 words · Act) — Echo the garden image. Final line: "Give the few hours. The neighbourhood is only the sum of them."

Model answer — Stem 1 (Q4-style: ~400 words) — letter to a newspaper — opens with a bold-statement hook

[I — Introduction]

Sir,

A community is not a place you live in; it is a thing you keep alive. We talk about our neighbourhoods as though they were weather — conditions that simply happen to us, improving or declining of their own accord. They are not. A community is the sum of the hours its members are willing to give it, and where those hours stop, the community quietly stops too.

[U — Urgency]

A neighbourhood is a garden, and an untended garden does not stay still — it returns to weeds. The National Council for Voluntary Organisations reports that formal monthly volunteering in England has fallen to just 16 per cent, down from 23 per cent before the pandemic, and the effects are visible on every high street: the library open three days instead of six, the green left to grow wild, the lunch club closed because the two people who ran it for a decade finally grew tired. This is not someone else's job. The someone else was always us.

[M — Methodology]

The remedy is unglamorous, and that is precisely why it works. It begins with a few hours: the Saturday-morning litter-pick, the hour spent hearing a younger child read, the lift given to a neighbour who can no longer drive to the surgery. None of these requires money; all of them require only that someone decides to begin. It continues with young hands — school-linked volunteering schemes build the habit early, and the teenager who helps at the food bank at fifteen is the volunteer who runs it at forty. And it depends, finally, on the institutions that survive on goodwill alone: the library, the food bank, the grassroots sports club that has never once been staffed by anyone paid to be there. These are not charities asking for our pity. They are the scaffolding of the place we live, and they are held up by hours.

[V — Vision]

The library opens on Saturdays again. The green is mown by the people who walk across it. The street remembers that it is a neighbourhood.

[C — Counter-argument]

Some will say volunteering is a privilege only the comfortable can afford. It is true that not everyone can spare the hours — and it is also true that a community waiting only for the council is a community already half-empty. Both are real; only one is a plan.

[C — Conclusion]

So I ask my neighbours, through your pages, for the smallest of commitments: the few hours a month that keep the garden from the weeds. The neighbourhood is only ever the sum of them.

Yours faithfully, [a resident]

Q7 — Personal heroes and admiration

Q7 — Personal heroes and admiration

Why this is likely. A 'someone you admire' talk is a recurring Eduqas Section B shape, well suited to the spoken-form Q3 task, and it pairs with Section A's two-century writing on reputation, character and public figures.

Three plausible stems:

  1. "Give a talk to your class about a person you admire and what their example teaches." Talk to your class.
  2. "Our real heroes are the people no one writes about." Write a talk to your class arguing for this view.
  3. "Teenagers should choose their heroes more carefully." Write an article giving your views.

Frame. A hero is not someone who is famous; a hero is someone whose ordinary choices, repeated, become extraordinary. The admiration that lasts is admiration for the work, not the fame — and the talk argues that the best heroes are often the unwatched ones.

I — INTRODUCTION (30–60 words · Focus) — Hooks: imagine scenario ("Picture the person who shaped you most — and notice they were probably never on a screen."); bold statement ("My hero has never been famous, and never will be."); contrast ("We are taught to admire the famous; we are shaped by the unwatched."). Bridge: admiration is for the work, not the fame.

U — URGENCY (60–90 words · Feel) — Metaphor: fame is a spotlight; character is the light that stays on when the spotlight moves away. Evidence: argue from concrete figures rather than a survey statistic — the teacher, the carer, the volunteer — and let the contrast between the celebrated and the genuinely admirable carry the point. Short pivot: "This is not about fame."

M — METHODOLOGY (150–220 words · Think) — M1, the ordinary made extraordinary: the nurse, the teacher, the parent — the repeated choice. M2, the test of a hero: not what they achieved but what they did when no one watched. M3, choosing well: admire the discipline, the kindness, the courage — not the follower count. Verbal bridges. Keep it personal and warm (talk register).

V — VISION (60–90 words · Feel + Act) — "We measure greatness by character, not by cameras. The unwatched are finally seen. The teenager admires the work."

C — COUNTER-ARGUMENT (45–60 words · Think) — three options — Aspiration (famous heroes inspire ambition); naivety (ordinary people are not 'heroes'); taste (let people admire whom they like). Pivot: "Fame can inspire — and it can also teach us to admire the wrong thing well. Both are possible; only one builds a character worth having."

C — CONCLUSION (45–60 words · Act) — Echo the unwatched figure. Final line: "Choose the quiet hero. Then become one."

Model answer — Stem 1 (Q3-style: ~400 words) — talk to your class — opens with an imagine-scenario hook

[I — Introduction]

Imagine the person who has shaped you most. Picture them clearly — and then notice something. They were almost certainly never on a screen. The people who change us are rarely the people we are taught to admire, and that gap, between the famous and the formative, is what I want to talk to you about today.

[U — Urgency]

Fame is a spotlight; character is the light that stays on when the spotlight moves away. We have grown up in a decade that confuses the two — that measures a person's worth by their follower count and treats attention as if it were achievement. But the people who actually built us did their building quietly, with no audience and no applause, and we have somehow learned to overlook exactly the kind of person we most need to become. This is not about fame.

[M — Methodology]

Think about what a hero really is. A hero is not someone who is famous; a hero is someone whose ordinary choices, repeated for years, become extraordinary. The nurse who works the night shift again, the teacher who stays behind to explain it one more time, the parent who goes without so a child does not have to — none of them trends, and all of them transform a life. There is a simple test for this. A hero is revealed not by what they achieve when the cameras are on them, but by what they do when no one is watching at all. And once you accept that, you start choosing your heroes more carefully: you admire the discipline rather than the fame, the kindness rather than the wealth, the courage rather than the reach. The follower count tells you who is watched. It tells you nothing about who is worth watching.

[V — Vision]

We measure greatness by character, not by cameras. The unwatched are finally seen for what they gave us. The teenager learns to admire the work, not the noise around it.

[C — Counter-argument]

Some of you will say famous heroes inspire real ambition — and sometimes they do. Fame can inspire; it can also teach us to admire the wrong thing very well. Both are possible; only one of them builds a character worth having.

[C — Conclusion]

So my challenge to you is two-part, and it is simple. Choose the quiet hero — the unwatched one who actually shaped you. And then, with the years you are given, become one.

Think about what a hero really is. A hero is not someone who is famous; a hero is someone whose ordinary choices, repeated for years, become extraordinary. The nurse who works the night shift again, the teacher who stays behind to explain it one more time, the parent who goes without so a child does not have to — none of them trends, and all of them transform a life. There is a simple test for this. A hero is revealed not by what they achieve when the cameras are on them, but by what they do when no one is watching at all. And once you accept that, you start choosing your heroes more carefully: you admire the discipline rather than the fame, the kindness rather than the wealth, the courage rather than the reach. The follower count tells you who is watched. It tells you nothing about who is worth watching.

[V — Vision]

We measure greatness by character, not by cameras. The unwatched are finally seen for what they gave us. The teenager learns to admire the work, not the noise around it.

[C — Counter-argument]

Some of you will say famous heroes inspire real ambition — and sometimes they do. Fame can inspire; it can also teach us to admire the wrong thing very well. Both are possible; only one of them builds a character worth having.

[C — Conclusion]

So my challenge to you is two-part, and it is simple. Choose the quiet hero — the unwatched one who actually shaped you. And then, with the years you are given, become one.

Q8 — Health, fitness and wellbeing

Q8 — Health, fitness and wellbeing

Why this is likely. Health and wellbeing is a live, statistically-grounded cluster well suited to the Q3 article, and it pairs with Section A's two-century writing on the body, exercise and daily life (Victorian health writing against 21st-century activity journalism).

Three plausible stems:

  1. "Write an article persuading teenagers to take their physical health more seriously." Article for the school or college magazine.
  2. "Wellbeing has become an industry. Real health was always free." Write an article giving your views.
  3. "Schools care more about exam results than about whether their students can run for a bus." Write an article for a magazine.

Frame. Health is not a luxury bolted on to a busy life; it is the platform every other part of life stands on. The argument is against treating movement as optional — and against an industry that sells back to us, at a price, the activity that was always free.

I — INTRODUCTION (30–60 words · Focus) — Hooks: rhetorical question ("When did you last move for an hour — not to get somewhere, just to move?"); counterintuitive fact ("More than a third of British five-to-sixteen-year-olds do not meet the recommended activity levels."); bold statement ("Your body is the only thing you are guaranteed to keep for life."). Bridge: health is the platform, not the luxury.

U — URGENCY (60–90 words · Feel) — Metaphor: the body as a house we live in but never maintain. Evidence: Sport England Active Lives 2023/24 — 52% of children aged 5–16 in England do not meet the recommended 60 minutes of daily activity; NHS guidance — 60 minutes a day for under-18s. Short pivot: "This is not vanity."

M — METHODOLOGY (150–220 words · Think) — M1, the free hour: the walk, the run, the kickabout — movement that costs nothing (against the wellbeing industry that sells it back). M2, the body and the mind: activity protects sleep and mood, not just muscle. M3, the habit, not the gym: small, daily, repeatable beats expensive and abandoned. Sport England Active Lives 2024; NHS guidance. Verbal bridges.

V — VISION (60–90 words · Feel + Act) — "The hour is taken back. The body is maintained, not neglected. The mind is steadier for it."

C — COUNTER-ARGUMENT (45–60 words · Think) — three options — Time (teenagers are too busy); genes (some people are just unfit); cost (fitness is expensive). Pivot: "There is no time, and there is the walk you could take instead of the scroll. Both are real; only one of them ends with a body you can rely on."

C — CONCLUSION (45–60 words · Act) — Echo the unmaintained house. Final line: "Move the free hour. Your body is the one thing you cannot replace."

Model answer — Stem 1 (Q3-style: ~400 words) — article for the school magazine — opens with a rhetorical-question hook

[I — Introduction]

When did you last move for an hour — not to get somewhere, not because you had to, but simply to move? For a lot of us the honest answer is that we cannot remember. Somewhere between the screens and the schoolwork, the body has quietly become the one thing we never make time for, and that is a strange order of priorities for the only thing we are guaranteed to keep for life.

[U — Urgency]

The body is a house we live in but never maintain. Sport England's 2024 Active Lives survey found that thirty-six percent of British five-to-sixteen-year-olds do not meet the recommended activity levels, against an NHS guideline of sixty minutes a day for under-eighteens. We would not let the house we lived in fall into disrepair for years and then act surprised when the roof came down. This is not vanity. It is maintenance, and we are skipping it.

[M — Methodology]

The good news is that the fix was never expensive. It begins with the free hour: the walk, the run, the kickabout in the park, the swim — movement that costs nothing and that the wellbeing industry has spent a fortune persuading us we need to buy back. Then there is what that hour actually protects. Activity does not only build muscle; it steadies the mind, defends sleep, and lifts mood more reliably than almost anything sold to do the same job. And the real secret is that it is the habit, not the gym, that wins. A daily twenty minutes, repeated for a year, will reshape a body that an expensive membership, bought in January and abandoned by March, never touches. The students who stay healthy are not the ones with the best equipment. They are the ones who simply kept moving.

[V — Vision]

The free hour is taken back. The body is maintained, not neglected. The mind is steadier for it.

[C — Counter-argument]

Some will say teenagers are simply too busy. There is no time — and there is the walk you could take instead of the scroll. Both are real; only one of them ends with a body you can actually rely on.

[C — Conclusion]

So take the free hour back. Walk, run, swim, play — it does not matter which. Your body is the one possession you cannot replace; maintain it now, while it is still easy.

Alternative stems. The same shell answers Stem 2 (article on the wellbeing industry) and Stem 3 (article on schools valuing results over fitness). The rhetorical-question hook fits all three.

Voice + banned-patterns check. House / maintenance metaphor anchored I → U → Conclusion. Sport England + NHS figures verified. No shows. ~400 words.

Q9 — Education and exam culture

Q9 — Education and exam culture

Why this is likely. Exam pressure and the volume of testing is a recurrent, contentious cluster ideal for the Q4 letter, and it pairs with Section A's two-century writing on schooling and childhood. The 2025 Curriculum and Assessment Review keeps it current.

Three plausible stems:

  1. "We test our children to exhaustion and call it ambition. The number of exams must come down." Write a letter to a newspaper giving your views.
  2. "Exams ruined nothing. The world rewards the people who can sit, focus and finish." Write a letter to a newspaper responding to this statement.
  3. "Education has forgotten that the point was never the exam." Write an article giving your views.

Frame. Schooling is not the enemy of childhood; the volume of testing is. A system that mistakes anxiety for ambition punishes the very students it claims to stretch. The reform target is the quantity of assessment, not its rigour.

I — INTRODUCTION (30–60 words · Focus) — Hooks: extended metaphor ("We have built an education that weighs the pig instead of feeding it."); counterintuitive fact ("Teenagers now sit dozens of exams between thirteen and eighteen."); bold statement ("Anxiety is not ambition, however much it looks like it."). Bridge: the volume, not the schooling, is the harm.

U — URGENCY (60–90 words · Feel) — Metaphor: the exam timetable as a treadmill that speeds up but never arrives. Evidence: NHS / Sleep Foundation 8–10 hours (against the revision that steals it); 2025 Curriculum and Assessment Review Interim Report (DfE, March 2025). Short pivot: "This is not learning."

M — METHODOLOGY (150–220 words · Think) — M1, the volume: fewer exams, same rigour — sample at the end, not every term (2025 Review). M2, the cost: the sleep debt and anxiety the calendar manufactures (NHS guidance). M3, the purpose restored: assessment that rewards thinking, not recall. Verbal bridges.

V — VISION (60–90 words · Feel + Act) — "The timetable holds the same standard in half the exams. The teenager sleeps. Learning replaces survival."

C — COUNTER-ARGUMENT (45–60 words · Think) — three options — Resilience (exams build it); rigour (fewer exams means lower standards); real-world (life is full of pressure). Pivot: "Pressure builds resilience — and relentless pressure builds burnout. Both are real; only one produces an adult who still wants to learn."

C — CONCLUSION (45–60 words · Act) — Echo the treadmill / weighed-pig image. Final line: "Reduce the volume. Keep the rigour. Feed the child, do not only weigh her."

Model answer — Stem 1 (Q4-style: ~400 words) — letter to a newspaper — opens with an extended-metaphor hook

[I — Introduction]

Sir,

There is an old saying among farmers that you do not fatten a pig by weighing it. We appear to have forgotten it. We have built an education system that weighs our children constantly — test after test, term after term — and then expresses surprise that the weighing alone has not made them grow. The volume of examination, not the schooling beneath it, is what has gone wrong.

[U — Urgency]

The exam timetable has become a treadmill that speeds up but never arrives. Teenagers now sit dozens of formal examinations between thirteen and eighteen, and the revision they demand eats directly into the eight to ten hours of sleep the NHS and the Sleep Foundation recommend for that age. The Department for Education's own 2025 Curriculum and Assessment Review Interim Report, published in March, finally said in public what teachers have said in private for a decade: that the breadth of testing has become dilution rather than rigour. This is not learning. It is measurement mistaken for teaching.

[M — Methodology]

The correction is not to lower standards; it is to stop confusing volume with value. The first step is the quantity itself: the same rigour can be sampled at the end of a course rather than re-measured every term, exactly as the 2025 Review proposes. The second is to count the cost we are already paying — the sleep debt, the anxiety, the capable students who arrive at their best exam too exhausted to sit it well, all of it manufactured by a calendar rather than by any genuine demand of the subject. The third is to remember what assessment was for. A test that rewards thinking, that asks a student what she makes of a problem rather than what she can recall about it, measures something worth measuring — and it does not need to be repeated forty times to do so. Keep the difficulty. Drop the repetition. The two were never the same thing.

[V — Vision]

The timetable holds the same standard inside half the examinations. The teenager sleeps. Learning, at last, replaces survival.

[C — Counter-argument]

Some will say exams build resilience, and that life is full of pressure. Pressure builds resilience — and relentless pressure builds burnout. Both are real; only one produces an adult who still wants to learn.

[C — Conclusion]

So I ask, through your pages, for the reform the Review has already named: fewer exams, the same rigour, a childhood that learns rather than merely survives. Feed the child. Do not only weigh her.

Yours faithfully, [a parent / a teacher / a sixth-form student]

Alternative stems. The same shell answers Stem 2 (letter defending exams — invert M1 so volume becomes the discipline) and Stem 3 (article on education forgetting its purpose). The extended-metaphor hook fits all three.

Voice + banned-patterns check. Weighed-pig / treadmill metaphor anchored I → U → Conclusion. 2025 Review and NHS guidance verified. No shows. ~400 words.

Q10 — Travel and favourite places

Q10 — Travel and favourite places

Why this is likely. A 'place you love / why travel matters' talk is a recurring Eduqas Section B shape, ideal for the spoken-form Q3 task, and it pairs directly with Section A's rich two-century travel-writing tradition (Victorian travel letters against 21st-century tourism journalism).

Three plausible stems:

  1. "Give a talk to your class about a place that means more to you than anywhere else, and why." Talk to your class.
  2. "We travel further than any generation in history, and arrive nowhere." Write a talk to your class arguing for this view.
  3. "The best journeys are the shortest ones." Write an article giving your views.

Frame. A place we love is not a destination ticked off a list; it is a relationship built by returning. The argument turns the modern instinct — travel more, travel further — on its head: the place that means most is usually the one we went back to, not the one we flew to once.

I — INTRODUCTION (30–60 words · Focus) — Hooks: repetition / anaphora ("There is a place. There is one place. There is a place I would choose over every airport in the world."); sensory snapshot (the specific shore, light, sound); contrast ("We are taught to admire the far; I want to defend the near."). Bridge: a loved place is a relationship, not a destination.

U — URGENCY (60–90 words · Feel) — Metaphor: the holiday list as a stamp collection — many stamps, no letters. Evidence: UNWTO 2024 — 1.4 billion international arrivals in 2023; Eurostar 2024 record demand. Short pivot: "This is not travel."

M — METHODOLOGY (150–220 words · Think) — M1, the place that returns: the spot you go back to until it knows you. M2, the slower journey: depth over distance — rail over flight (Eurostar 2024); one place known beats ten skimmed. M3, the carbon and the cost: the far trip is dearer in both senses; the near one outlasts it. UNWTO 2024. Verbal bridges. Keep it warm (talk register).

V — VISION (60–90 words · Feel + Act) — "We return until a place knows us. We travel slower, and arrive more completely. The near place outlasts the far one."

C — COUNTER-ARGUMENT (45–60 words · Think) — three options — Horizons (travel broadens the mind); opportunity (see the world while you can); privilege (staying near is for those who have already travelled). Pivot: "Travel broadens the mind — and a hundred skimmed cities narrow it again into a blur. Both are real; only one of them you will still remember at fifty."

C — CONCLUSION (45–60 words · Act) — Echo the one place. Final line: "Go back to the place you love. Let it know you."

Model answer — Stem 1 (Q3-style: ~400 words) — talk to your class — opens with a repetition / anaphora hook

[I — Introduction]

There is a place. There is one place I would choose over every airport in the world. It is not far, it is not famous, and you have almost certainly never heard of it — and that, I think, is exactly why it matters. A place you truly love is never a destination you ticked off a list. It is a relationship, and relationships are built by returning.

[U — Urgency]

The modern holiday has become a stamp collection: many stamps, no letters. The UN World Tourism Organization recorded over 1.4 billion international arrivals in 2023, and Eurostar reported record demand in 2024 — we are travelling further than any generation in history. And yet ask anyone to describe the city they spent two days in last summer, and they reach for the photographs, because the place itself never had time to leave a mark. This is not travel. It is collecting.

[M — Methodology]

So let me tell you what I have learned about places. First, the place that means most is the one you return to — the shore, the wood, the street that, after enough visits, finally begins to know you back. You cannot rush that; it is built only by going again. Second, the slower journey is the deeper one. A single coastline walked a dozen times will give you more than a dozen coastlines glimpsed once from a coach window, and the train that takes you there, at a fraction of a flight's carbon, gives you the journey as well as the arrival. Third — and this is the part we rarely admit — the far trip costs more in every sense: more money, more carbon, more exhaustion, and somehow less memory. The near place asks little and returns everything. The far place asks everything and returns a photograph.

[V — Vision]

We return until a place knows us. We travel slower, and we arrive more completely. The near place we love outlasts the far place we merely visited.

[C — Counter-argument]

Some of you will say travel broadens the mind — and it can. A hundred skimmed cities can also narrow it again into a single grey blur. Both are real; only one of them you will still remember clearly at fifty.

[C — Conclusion]

So here is my advice, and it costs nothing. Find the place you love — the near one, the unfamous one — and go back to it, again and again, until it knows you.

Alternative stems. The same shell answers Stem 2 (talk on travelling further but arriving nowhere) and Stem 3 (article on the shortest journeys being the best). The repetition hook fits the talk especially well.

Cross-question reuse table — memorise the recombinations

The ten plans above share underlying moves. The same metaphor frame, hook archetype, or refutation pivot reappears across multiple Qs. Memorise the recombinations and you can rebuild any plan from any other on the day — which matters doubly here, because Section B asks for two pieces from the same toolkit.

Move Used in Reusable in
Slot-machine / engineered-loss / extraction metaphor Q1 (Screen time) Q4 (Celebrity), Q8 (Health)
Machine / flood-already-inside metaphor Q2 (Environment) Q9 (Exam culture), Q3 (Consumer)
Warehouse / accumulation-vs-having framing Q3 (Consumer attachment) Q10 (Travel), Q8 (Health)
Hall-of-mirrors / industrial-comparison framing Q4 (Celebrity) Q1 (Screen time), Q3 (Consumer)
Pass-through-vs-move-in / inhabit framing Q5 (School advice) Q7 (Heroes), Q10 (Travel)
Garden / tended-vs-weeds framing Q6 (Community) Q2 (Environment), Q8 (Health)
Spotlight-vs-quiet-light / character framing Q7 (Heroes) Q5 (School advice), Q4 (Celebrity)
House / maintenance-vs-neglect metaphor Q8 (Health) Q5 (School advice), Q3 (Consumer)
Weighed-pig / treadmill metaphor Q9 (Exam culture) Q5 (School advice), Q2 (Environment)
Stamp-collection / relationship-vs-tick-list framing Q10 (Travel) Q3 (Consumer), Q7 (Heroes)
Counterintuitive opening fact Q1, Q4, Q8 Any
Circular Conclusion echoing Introduction image All ten Mandatory shape across all 10
Triadic structure with short-sentence pivot All ten Mandatory shape across all 10

Rehearse for two tasks, not one. Because Q3 and Q4 are both compulsory, pick a talk/article shape (Q3) and a letter shape (Q4) and rehearse one of each until you can produce both in roughly twenty-five minutes each. If time is tight, rehearse Q1, Q5 and Q9 first — a letter, an article and a letter that between them cover the most probable 2026 themes. The shells are deliberately recombinant: on the day, the metaphor frame you rehearsed for one theme may fit the stem Eduqas actually prints for the other.

Creative stretches — one extreme example per Q

The ten skeletons above are the SAFE shape — IUMVCC, six beats, the locked structure that consistently scores in the top AO5 band. In a 400-word Eduqas task they will land your answer firmly in the upper bands.

To push to the very top, the examiner needs to see voice. Not just techniques — voice. One Sophicly student once wrote a film review of The Hulk in The Hulk's own voice; the inventive form was a perfect AO5 reward, because the form FIT the topic. None of the ideas below is a template to copy. They are proof that the opening can be anything, provided it earns its place — and in 400 words a stretch must be even tighter, because there is no room to recover if the conceit collapses.

The rule for breaking the rules. A creative stretch only works if the voice / form choice fits the topic. Always ask: does this voice ARGUE the topic, or does it just decorate it? If it argues, ship it. If it decorates, cut it.

Below, one stretch idea per Q. Use them as inspiration; do NOT mimic them verbatim. In a two-task paper, attempt a stretch on one task at most — keep the other safe.

  • Q1 — Screen time: write the letter as if dictated by the phone itself, confessing what it took. Keep it short and dry; let the confession do the argument.
  • Q2 — Environment: frame the talk as a 2050 weather forecast read aloud, the catastrophic numbers delivered in a presenter's cheerful register, broken at the end by the presenter stepping out of character.
  • Q3 — Consumer attachment: write the article as an inventory read aloud — each object named, then the one object you would save, the contrast carrying the point.
  • Q4 — Celebrity: open with three lines of glossy influencer caption, then cut to the real voice: "That post took four seconds to write and a year off someone's confidence to believe."
  • Q5 — School advice: structure the article as a letter to your own first-day self — what you wish you had known, written with the warmth of hindsight.
  • Q6 — Community: personify the local library, narrating the Saturdays it used to open and the volunteers who kept it alive — breaking to a call to action at the close.
  • Q7 — Heroes: open the talk by describing your hero without naming them, so the class assumes you mean someone famous — then reveal it is a relative or a teacher.
  • Q8 — Health: write the article from the point of view of the body itself, keeping a quiet ledger of the hours it was given and the hours it was denied.
  • Q9 — Exam culture: open the letter as a parody of an exam rubric ("You have ninety minutes. Do not turn over until told.") before breaking into the human argument.
  • Q10 — Travel: personify the one beloved place, addressing the class directly: "You have never been to me. Most of you never will. Let me tell you why that is your loss, not mine."

How to use these stretches in the exam. - Do not attempt a stretch unless you have rehearsed it once. Cold improvisation across two timed tasks is risky. - The IUMVCC beats stay. Even the most inventive persona piece still has an introduction, an argument, a counter-argument, and a conclusion. - One stretch only, across both tasks. Pick one task to stretch; keep the other safe. - If the topic doesn't fit, ship the safe skeleton. A controlled upper-band answer beats a confused experimental one every time.

Final pre-submission checklist (run it TWICE — once per task)

Before you put the pen down on each piece, run this checklist. Every item costs marks if it slips. Remember: you are doing this for both Q3 and Q4.

  • ✓ Six IUMVCC beats present and named in order.
  • ✓ Word count around 400 per task. Much under 400 = AO5 penalty; much over and you risk running out of time for the second task.
  • ✓ Both tasks attempted and complete. An unfinished or missing task loses up to 20 marks on its own.
  • ✓ The two tasks are in different forms / registers (one article or talk, one letter) where the prompts allow.
  • ✓ Hook from one of the eight archetypes — not a generic opening — and a different archetype for each task.
  • ✓ One extended metaphor anchored across at least three beats (I → U → Conclusion at minimum).
  • ✓ Triadic structure used with parallel grammar at least once.
  • ✓ Conclusion echoes the Introduction image (circular structure rewards AO5).
  • ✓ Direct address (you, we, us) used for solidarity, not scattered mechanically.
  • ✓ Every statistic real; any gap marked and avoided rather than invented.
  • ✗ No shows / showing / shown.
  • ✗ No abstract concept nouns (the importance of, the situation with, the necessity to).
  • ✗ No discourse-marker scaffolding (Firstly, Secondly, Finally).
  • ✗ No really, very, just, basically.
  • ✗ No Americanisms (move as noun, gotten, trash).
  • ✗ Fewer than six to be verbs (is, are, was, were) per piece.
  • ✗ No more than three or four imperatives per piece.
  • ✗ No exclamation marks outside quoted speech. No arrows in prose.

Tick every line, on both pieces. Section B is forty marks across two tasks. Two controlled ~400-word pieces, each running IUMVCC underneath, is the whole game.
